title: Beam Search: Keeping Top Candidates
concept: Beam search maintains a fixed number (beam width B) of the most likely partial sentences at each step, balancing speed and quality.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of Greedy Search?

options.text: It only keeps the single best choice at each step.
It looks at all possible sentences in the world.

exercises.explain: Greedy search only looks one step ahead and picks the single best choice immediately, without looking at other promising options.

cards.title: The Translation Balance
cards.body: When translation AIs guess the next word, looking at only the absolute best option can lead to mistakes later. But looking at every single option takes forever! How do AIs find the perfect middle ground? They use a spotlight approach.

cards.title: What is Beam Search?
cards.body: Beam Search is a smart translation strategy. Instead of looking at only one word at a time, or looking at every single combination in the dictionary, it keeps track of a fixed number of top candidates (called the Beam Width, or B) at every step. This balances speed and quality perfectly.

cards.title: The Flashlight Team
cards.body: Imagine searching a dark, branching cave with flashlight beams. Greedy search is like having only one thin flashlight beam—you just follow the single brightest spot, even if it leads to a dead end. Beam search is like having three flashlights (a beam width of 3). You can shine light down the three most promising paths at the same time, finding a much better way out!

cards.title: Beam Search in Action
cards.body:
Suppose we translate a sentence with a Beam Width of 2:
1. At the first word, we keep the top 2 best starting words: "The" and "A".
2. Next, we look at all next words for both. We get combinations like "The cat", "The dog", "A cat", "A toy".
3. We calculate the scores and keep only the top 2 overall (e.g., "The cat" and "The dog").
4. We repeat this until we reach the end!

cards.title: Is Bigger Always Better?
cards.body: Many people think that a larger beam width always makes translation perfect. While a wider beam (like keeping 10 candidates instead of 2) can find better sentences, it also makes the AI run much slower and use more memory. Finding the right balance is key!

cards.title: Search Comparison
cards.body: Let's compare how Greedy Search and Beam Search look at translation choices.

left.text: Keeps only 1 path. Fast, but easily gets stuck if the first word choice was a mistake.

right.text: Keeps 2 paths. Explores multiple directions at once, leading to better translations.

exercises.prompt: How does Beam Search decide which translations to keep track of?

options.text: It checks every single combination of words in the dictionary.
It keeps a fixed number of the most likely partial sentences at each step.
It only looks at the very last word of a sentence.
It randomly picks words to translate.

exercises.explain: Beam search keeps a small, fixed number of top paths at each step. This keeps it fast while still exploring multiple good options, preventing it from getting stuck on a single bad path.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Increasing the beam width makes the AI translate sentences faster.
exercises.explain: A larger beam width means the AI tracks more possibilities at the same time. This requires more computations, which makes the translation process slower.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how we limit our search:
exercises.sentence: The number of active candidate sentences we keep at each step is called the beam width.
exercises.explain: The beam width (B) is the fixed number of top translation paths that the AI keeps track of at each step.

exercises.prompt: Which of these systems is using Beam Search?

options.text: System A: "I only look at the single absolute best word at each step and ignore all other options."
System B: "I keep the top 2 best sentence paths at each step and update them as I go."
System C: "I pick random words from the dictionary and see if they sound good."

exercises.explain: System B is using Beam Search because it maintains a set of top candidates (beam width of 2) at each step. System A is using Greedy Search because it only keeps the single best word. System C is just picking words randomly.

exercises.prompt: Imagine our Beam Search has a beam width (B) of 3. At step 2, we expand our top paths and end up with 4 possible candidate sentences. What does Beam Search do next?

options.text: It keeps all 4 paths because they all started from good words.
It compares all 4 paths and drops the one with the lowest score, keeping the top 3.
It only looks at the paths starting with 'The cat' and ignores 'The dog'.
It stops translating immediately because 4 paths is too many.

exercises.explain: A beam width of 3 means at each step we only keep the top 3 overall best sequences. When we expand 'The cat' (which has two paths) and 'The dog' (which has two paths), we get 4 candidate paths in total. We sort them all by score and discard the worst one, keeping only the top 3.
